speaker: interviewer
text: November the 22nd, '94. Survivor is Gertrude Weiss. Interviewer is Sandy Jacobson. It's in Los Angeles. And it's in English. And it is third tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into your life in Auschwitz, I would like you to please explain about the boxcars. You were in one with your father and your sister. And your mother had become separated. Your mother and your other siblings had become separated. Did they-- did the Germans, when you would arrive, did they open each boxcar individually, while other-- and another one would wait? The other cars would wait while each one was being opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankly, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when you say that your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think that there were several open at once because--

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a throng.

speaker: interviewee
text: --people [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so now you are separated from your father. And you and your sister are taken to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are taken-- we don't know where, to a building. First, we are stripped off of everything. I also, something that I'll never forget, I took along a souvenir book. It was customary at that time that friends-- it was a beautiful book with dark red Bordeaux velvet cover. And your friends wrote in little poems and signed it, you know, addressed to you, which was in school and on an occasion of graduations and things. And that was something what, personally as a young girl, it was very precious to me. So I took it along, so-- and I schlepped it all the way. And as I tell you, we wore dresses and coats and all kind of things. And so first, we had to take off everything. Of course-- we were-- we were undressed, completely nude. And we were taken into-- again, I cannot tell you what I felt. We were herded like-- like cattle. I cannot tell you exactly who was with me, except my sister. I don't know. We were-- we were a group. I don't-- I can't tell you how many. There was no time for anything. You were pushed by these soldiers, by these people-- that's what-- and dressed completely nude. And then some water opened from the ceiling, a bunch of shower heads from the ceiling over you with hot water, with a [? little-- ?] small pieces of soap, and a-- a terrible smell. It was a disinfectant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After the-- after this, we were herded into another room, everything still nude. There was all the soldiers, was everybody. And we were passing one by one. And we were handed from a big pile of-- of clothes, which were thrown together like a mountain of clothes, we were handed a dress. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Pardon me, no. First, after the shower, we were taken into a room where we were-- our hair was cut completely. That was one of the first nightmares. I was handed a little dress, a little white cotton dress, with some red I remember, which was long. Everything was long for me, of course. But I-- as soon as I-- I-- we got into our block, I-- somehow I tore off a piece, and I covered my head. Nothing was so cold as my head. I shall never forget that. That shivering, my head was the worse in the cold. Again, I don't know if you could picture that, how I looked. A young girl. I wasn't very developed-- bald. I had freckles. But the-- the-- the misery of that shaven head is unforgettable. So firstly, but after the baths, we were shaven, and then we were handed these dresses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you about being bald. Was it a-- a thing of being a young woman who has lost what was considered a woman's crowning glory, her hair? Was it a religious thing? What-- what was it that was so shaming to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sorry to tell you, it wasn't shame. It was pure suffering, misery. Who could think of shame of-- or of losing your-- your crown or what? Those were not normal circumstances. You had to understand, that was something, the instinct of survival, and-- and the pain and the suffering of cold, of being exposed, your head, which was never exposed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was really-- that's all it was. You didn't care about anything else?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time? Nothing. Maybe months later. But at that time? And the whole confusion, we were just ripped away from our parents and from everybody. And we didn't know what happening, except something like-- we never-- never read about something like that, not to experience or to think or to hear.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you had talked about the rumors, you would hear rumors in the ghetto and even before. Nothing like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course not. This was-- this was the world's best kept secret. Nobody ever heard of it. Although the-- the Slovak Jews were taken much sooner. But never, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at least not us in-- in Hungary or not us in Satu Mare. They said, they are-- we are going to be taken to some-- we didn't think that we are going to be taken out of the country. In the country, to some place, because-- to-- to work for the war effort. We are, you know, like the Japanese or here, we were not people who were trusted. And-- and they put us to work, to have forced, free labor. But not something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you're there with your sister in a-- talk about the barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now-- then we are-- we were taken into a barrack, which was a long wooden building, quite a few. We were-- we were taken into the C lager. And we were housed in some bunks, bunk beds like. The-- the months in Auschwitz, how many months were they? From June until October-- the end of October was. The months in Auschwitz passed with being woken up at dawn and dragged-- taken out from the barracks, and lined up in front of the barracks, and stood there for hours. It was a form of torture. It was cold. It was a climate, sometimes during the day [INAUDIBLE]. But the early mornings were cold like-- and I tell you, again, with my shaven head, it was something. We were standing-- we were lined up, I guess in rows of six or seven. And you had to stay straight and put your best face on everything before-- because if you were noticed that you-- you were were weak or you were sick or you didn't feel good, you were in mortal danger. You were taken out, and never heard of again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister, who was always very thin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we took here between two-- she stood between us. I stood on one side and a cousin on the other side. And we kind of propped her up, because many times she-- she couldn't stay for hours. It was called Zahlappell during the appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got that-- that food, which was soup like you gave for the pigs. You know. For a while I couldn't eat it. For a while I couldn't. But after, I had to.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I can't-- these months passed in-- at C lager in Auschwitz was this. And with the-- every-- every few days, there was a-- a special Zahlappell when Mengele came. He inspected-- he inspected us and pulled out those who were not-- who seemed to him not fit. And then there were these selections for some labor camps. During those months, there were many transports taken from Auschwitz into different labor camps. I don't remember the different names. But we were not selected for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, let me interject here something. I-- I guess I mentioned that we were a very large family. We had a great grandmother living while I was a child. She was the great grandmother of [? Bessie ?] Weiss also, my-- because we were second cousins. But I want to tell you that-- so these-- one of these-- so we went to Batarci during summer, which I told you was such a treat. And I had there aunts and uncles. One aunt was the rabbi-- the daughter of a rabbi from Slovakia. And she married my uncle. And they lived in Batarci. But their oldest daughter was taken by her parents to be-- to be brought up in Slovakia and Bratislava. And she went to school in Czechoslovakia. And she was perfect German and Slovak.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was elected to work in the office, the [GERMAN], it's called. [GERMAN] is writing, [GERMAN] is room in German. So she had knowledge of these transports, when they are gonna be and where they are going. So there were-- there was some information where you are taken and what kind of work these-- these people are taken to. So through her help, we got-- she maneuvered that we should be in that transport. We should be selected in this transport, which went to the town, which was considered a very good transport, a very good labor camp, because you didn't have to work outdoors. It was an aeroplane factory. And now, again, retrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the selection processes in Auschwitz, those who were not fit by Mengele were pulled out. And they were locked in and-- I have to interject again something. Each block, each barrack of this was-- was managed by two girls, Jewish girls, but Jewish girls from Slovakia, who unfortunately were in Auschwitz many years before. And they were already trained by the Germans. And they had to work along with the Germans. That's how they survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were the ones who were in actual contact with us as to what to do. And-- So she had a-- like a little office, a little room of her own in-- in that barrack. We were in open, you know, but she had a little room. So during these selections, the ones which Mengele found not fit were thrown into this little room, and then later taken, you know, to the crematorium. Once my sister was taken out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were, of course, just-- but through Angela-- that was her name, Angela-- she smuggled her out. She-- she was-- she knew the lageralteste, it was called. The-- So you're cousin, who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Cousin who was working with the [GERMAN] was-- smuggled her out from there. And that's how she survived. And she also smuggled her like a-- today you would call it a sweatsuit, you know, a sweat-- you know, something heavy, pants, you know, which were thick. She should look more-- more of a-- of a mensch because she was very thin. And that's how we managed to stay together and to get to Zittau.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this cousin arranged for you to be-- so the selection for the labor camps wasn't made like, you to the right and you to the left. It was made with some thought, with some-- there were-- or there were plans made.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- yes, there were plans made. There were different-- different lagers. We were in the C lager. They were different lager. So not each time was the selection done by-- from the same barrack, understand? And-- and so you could manipulate somewhat if you had-- I tell you, we were lucky because we had her, and she had some inside knowledge and some. And this was really a miracle, my sister's survival. It was a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you and your sister, and your cousin as well, was she also selected for Zittau?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came-- she-- yes, she put herself in the [GERMAN], because it was almost a less transport, and it was a good one.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: In train-- in trains again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in trains we were taken to Zittau. I do not remember how long the train ride took. We arrived there and-- to a railway station. And we were taken, we were housed right away into a-- a regular building, not a-- not a barrack, about three stories. And it was like a-- like a dormitory type thing. It wasn't a fancy building of course, but it was like a dormitory thing. We were together, 12-- 12 girls in a-- in a room. And we were very fortunate because we were seven cousins in the same room. This cousin, whom I mentioned, a sister of hers, and other cousins. There was even a  mother. She was a few years younger than my mother. They were first cousins, who survived. Evidently, she looked young or she was lucky. She survived with her two daughters, and she was also in there. Today she is 95, and she's still mentally-- her-- her intellect is-- is fantastic. To talk with her is a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind work did you do at Zittau?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were assembling airplanes. We were mostly screwing things in with long screwdrivers, you know, which-- which a woman's hands, fingers, were-- were easier to reach than a man's. It was operated by the Wehrmacht, not by the SS. And we were working about four or five girls under one master mechanic. I guess he was a mechanical engineer, who was also a Wehrmacht, and relatively a decent man. He sometimes brought us an apple, something to eat. We had some conversations with him. And it was somewhat of a human connection, a human touch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were conditions there wretched? Or-- I mean, did you have more food there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we more-- we had different food. We had just, you know, in the morning some soup, and then we were given a piece of bread with a piece of margarine. Or they had those pieces of jelly like things. And then in the evening, we had something. Because the cook was a Jewish woman. You know. So it was-- it was very different than Auschwitz, of course. But Auschwitz was a terrible-- Auschwitz was a concentration camp. And this was a-- a work camp. And in order to work, you had to eat something, you know. So that's where we were. In fact, our-- our room was decorated with pictures because one of the girls was a girl from Budapest, an artist who did-- did very well in Auschwitz too because she drew portraits of all these SS men and things. And she-- and she got to-- to be part of our transport, because as I tell you, it was-- they heard that this was a good transport. And we were fortunate to get into it. [INAUDIBLE] was there with me too. We were fortunate to get into it-- we because of Gela.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when you-- when you said that the girl from Budapest painted-- did paintings, were they paintings about the war, or about life before? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I remember, vividly, portraits. She did a lot of portraits of-- of-- of each other, of us. And-- because mainly in-- in-- in Auschwitz, and even in Zittau, you know, these German wanted their portraits done. So that's what she did. That's I remember. The other pictures I do not remember. Only I remember a very pretty, petite girl with a big talent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- what they call block alteste?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have that in-- in Zittau as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a little bit more personal freedom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were 12 in a-- and then the Germans, you know. It was a much smaller group than-- than in-- in Auschwitz. And we had the Germans who escorted us every morning to the factory. We had to walk to the factory, also in columns. But it wasn't very far.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in regular clothes at that time, or in a uniform of some kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, regular clothes, what they handed us [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, when you talk about the SS or the Wehrmacht, you were young women, though you certainly didn't look your best. Were you ever bothered by--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I'm not surprised, looking the way I looked. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is October of '44 that you go to Zittau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed in Zittau from October of '44--

speaker: interviewee
text: Till May.

speaker: interviewer
text: --till May. And you were liberated in-- how-- how did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened terribly, because we were liberated by the Russians. Those who were fortunate to be liberated by the Allied forces was an entirely different story. We were just abandoned, left on our own. And by that time, many of us were sick and weak. You know, you were given some food, but as the months went on, you-- we didn't get any stronger. So we were abandoned. Just-- we woke up. We heard some rumors before, a few days. And we just woke up. There were no guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was as far-- that was all you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no guards.

speaker: interviewer
text: No celebration, no announcement, nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you kidding, the Russians-- nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we were-- some of us went into-- went into the city. We were-- we were on the outskirts, you know, the factory. But there there was a city. We went-- the-- the homes were abandoned. They were-- they fled. And we tried to take some food and some-- some clothes. And that was a mistake. The clothes it wasn't a mistake. But the clothes that I got, short pair of pants, which was, even then-- you know, the Germans wore short pants and those lederhosen, but-- and-- and a pullover. I was flat. Before that, I want to say, but after these months, you know. You know that our menstruation was stopped. It could not be handled under those circumstances. And-- and-- and with my shaven head, I looked like a not good looking little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but from the food, what we started to eat, different food, there were a lot of illness. There was typhoid. I became yellow. I got a kind of hepatitis. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- but I recovered from it. But let me tell you, so we were liberated. And although we knew what happened in-- in Auschwitz in the crematoriums, we still hoped against hope that we will find somebody. So our thing was going home. But there was-- we were weak. We had nothing. We had nobody. And it was after the war. There was no arrangement. There was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you get home? Because I don't know how. There were some who were quite ingenious, you know. And I don't know how they got hold of a wagon and-- and horses. Not-- not a carriage, a wagon. And we got to the railway station because we couldn't walk it. And over there, we sat about three days until we could-- were able to get on-- not on a-- not on a-- a [INAUDIBLE]-- a, you know, a train where-- where people traveled-- Passenger.

speaker: interviewee
text: --but not-- such-- such a common word, and it escapes me. Not on a passenger train, but on a-- we were just-- you know, and people were hanging outside the train. And there was no schedule, nothing. You went where the train went. And it went for a certain time, and it stopped. And here we are, somewhere, some obscure railway station. We were waiting for another train. It took weeks until we got home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you eat during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we took some food along, but I don't remember details. I don't remember details. I was sick by that time. I had hepatitis. So I was lying there. We came home with this cousin of my mother's. And I didn't eat much food. I suppose I needed some fluid, but I-- anyway, I got home, but sick. We got home with my sister. But I want to tell you, this cousin, Angela, Gela, who-- who was so helpful to us in the [NON-ENGLISH], came also back. And there were a lot of tragedies occurred because of the Russians, because the Russians did not organize, did not take care of anything of ours. We were left of our-- our own sick and-- and-- and helpless, and nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and-- we got to Budapest. And she, from Budapest, got on a Russian truck with Russian soldiers who were supposed to come to Satu Mare. They lived-- they lived next to Satu Mare, between Batarci and Satu Mare in a smaller town, Halmi-- Halmeu is what it's called. And she never arrived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I guess I have goose pimples. You don't know what--

speaker: interviewee
text: She never arrived home. Once we heard from somebody maybe they-- they-- they sighted her somewhere in Russia, but she never got home, that girl. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So-- All right, this is a good place. Let's change the tape.
